verses.text: Elkanah has two wives — Peninnah with children, Hannah with none. Already we feel the ache of a divided home and an empty womb. Scripture often begins its great works in a place of barrenness and longing, where only God can open what is closed.

Elkanah has two wives — Peninnah with children, Hannah with none. Already we feel the ache of a divided home and an empty womb. Scripture often begins its great works in a place of barrenness and longing, where only God can open what is closed.

Year by year Elkanah worships at Shiloh and gives Hannah a double portion, for he loves her though she is childless. His tenderness is real, but human love cannot fill the void only God can. A husband's kindness can ease the grief it cannot cure.

Year by year Elkanah worships at Shiloh and gives Hannah a double portion, for he loves her though she is childless. His tenderness is real, but human love cannot fill the void only God can. A husband's kindness can ease the grief it cannot cure.

Year by year Elkanah worships at Shiloh and gives Hannah a double portion, for he loves her though she is childless. His tenderness is real, but human love cannot fill the void only God can. A husband's kindness can ease the grief it cannot cure.

Peninnah provokes Hannah year after year till she weeps and will not eat. Elkanah pleads, 'am not I better to thee than ten sons?' — well meant, but beside the point. Some sorrows are too deep for any comfort but God's, and they drive us at last to prayer.

Peninnah provokes Hannah year after year till she weeps and will not eat. Elkanah pleads, 'am not I better to thee than ten sons?' — well meant, but beside the point. Some sorrows are too deep for any comfort but God's, and they drive us at last to prayer.

Peninnah provokes Hannah year after year till she weeps and will not eat. Elkanah pleads, 'am not I better to thee than ten sons?' — well meant, but beside the point. Some sorrows are too deep for any comfort but God's, and they drive us at last to prayer.

Hannah rises and pours out her soul to the LORD, vowing that if He gives her a son she will give him back. This is the prayer of real faith: bringing the exact grief to God, and holding the longed-for gift with an open hand even before it comes.

Eli watches her lips move without sound and assumes she is drunk. She answers gently: 'I have poured out my soul before the LORD.' Even God's ministers can misread a breaking heart. Hannah does not retaliate but quietly explains — grace under unjust suspicion.

Eli watches her lips move without sound and assumes she is drunk. She answers gently: 'I have poured out my soul before the LORD.' Even God's ministers can misread a breaking heart. Hannah does not retaliate but quietly explains — grace under unjust suspicion.

Eli blesses her, 'the God of Israel grant thy petition,' and Hannah 'went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad.' She had no child yet, only God's word — but faith let her rise and eat in peace, resting before the answer arrived.

Eli blesses her, 'the God of Israel grant thy petition,' and Hannah 'went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad.' She had no child yet, only God's word — but faith let her rise and eat in peace, resting before the answer arrived.

The LORD remembers Hannah, and she bears a son. She names him Samuel — 'asked of God' — for she had asked him of the LORD. Every child is a gift, but this one is a living monument to answered prayer, his very name a testimony that God hears.

The LORD remembers Hannah, and she bears a son. She names him Samuel — 'asked of God' — for she had asked him of the LORD. Every child is a gift, but this one is a living monument to answered prayer, his very name a testimony that God hears.

Elkanah goes up to worship, but Hannah stays home until the boy is weaned, meaning to keep her vow. She will not rush to surrender Samuel, nor go back on her word. Faith both treasures the gift and stays faithful to the promise made to God.

Elkanah goes up to worship, but Hannah stays home until the boy is weaned, meaning to keep her vow. She will not rush to surrender Samuel, nor go back on her word. Faith both treasures the gift and stays faithful to the promise made to God.

When he is weaned, Hannah brings the little boy to Eli: 'I have lent him to the LORD; as long as he liveth he shall be lent.' She gives back her dearest, hardest-won treasure. True dedication offers God not our leftovers but our most costly joys.
